feedback.text: I like the direction of the Digital Fairness Act. With the rising power of the digital private businesses and conglomerates, regulation is needed now more necessary than ever. I would like to propose an expansion of the law to also include protection of video games from being shutdown, essentially destroyed, by their own creators. An increasing number of video games are designed to rely on a server the publisher controls in order for the game to function. This acts as a lifeline to the game. When the publisher decides to turn this off, it is essentially cutting off life support to the game, making it completely inoperable for all customers. Companies that do this often intentionally prevent people from 'repairing' the game also by withholding vital components. When this happens, the game is 'destroyed', because no one can ever operate it again. There are two main reasons why I think that the EU should intervene and regulate this matter. First is about the consumer rights: The consumers have paid to get access to the game and with a unilateral decision from the seller, the game is being taken away from the buyer. The dark patterns that the Digital Fairness Act will be addressing apply also to the marketing and selling of video games. The second reason is that digital games are an art form, combining technology and all the classical arts, and they should be protected as such. From an ethical point-of-view we should give the public a chance to preserve the art piece. If the video game publishers keep killing games, these art pieces are being destroyed forever, and we are denying the next generations the right to experience these pieces of art. This isnt about asking publishers to support their games forever. Publishers should be able to end support for a game whenever they choose. What I am asking for is that they implement an end-of-life plan to modify or patch the game so that it can run on customer systems with no further support from the company being necessary. It is unrealistic to expect companies to support games indefinitely. This regulation would not interfere with any business activity whatsoever while the game was actively being supported. The regulations we are seeking would only apply when companies decide to end support for games. At that time, they would need to be converted to have either offline or private hosting modes. Until then, companies could continue running games any way they see fit. Additionally, there are already real-world examples of publishers ending support for online-only games in a responsible way, such as: - 'Gran Turismo Sport' published by Sony - 'Knockout City' published by Velan Studios - 'Mega Man X DiVE' published by Capcom - 'Scrolls / Caller's Bane' published by Mojang AB - 'Duelyst' published by Bandai Namco Entertainment This kind of request is in line with the Stop Destroying Videogames European Citizens' Initiative, which gathered over 1,4 million signatures. EU law can put a stop to this practice and allow society to preserve this art form.
